example  
another example  

the probability that the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in version b is less than the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in version a can be calculated using the central limit theorem. the mean interaction time for version a is 45 seconds with a standard deviation of 5 seconds, and for version b it is 40 seconds with a standard deviation of 3 seconds. the sample sizes are large enough to apply the central limit theorem, so the sampling distributions of the averages will be approximately normal. the probability that the average interaction time for version b is less than version a can be calculated by finding the z-score for the difference in means and then using the standard normal distribution to find the corresponding probability. the z-score is (45 - 40) / sqrt((5^2)/30 + (3^2)/30) = 5 / sqrt(2.5 + 0.3) = 5 / sqrt(2.8) ≈ 5 / 1.673 ≈ 3.0. the probability that the average interaction time for version b is less than version a is approximately 0.9987.
